stem: Evaluate the infinite geometric series \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} ([[a]])\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{k} \), which has first term \( [[a]] \) and common ratio \( \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \) with \( \left|\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right| < 1 \).
solution: For \( |r| < 1 \), \( \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} a r^{k} = \dfrac{a}{1-r} \). Here \( a = [[a]] \) and \( r = \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \), so the sum is \( \dfrac{[[a]]}{1 - \frac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \dfrac{a}{1+r} \); the geometric-sum formula has \( 1-r \) in the denominator.
This multiplies by \( (1-r) \) instead of dividing by it.
This drops the first term \( a \), using \( \dfrac{1}{1-r} \).
This is the sum starting at \( k=1 \) (first term \( ar \)), i.e. \( \dfrac{ar}{1-r} \).
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.

solution: The finite geometric sum formula gives \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N} ar^{k} = a\dfrac{1-r^{N+1}}{1-r} \). Here \( a=[[a]] \), \( r=\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \), \( N=[[N]] \), so the sum is \( [[a]]\cdot\dfrac{1-([[p]]/[[q]])^{[[N]]+1}}{1-[[p]]/[[q]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the INFINITE sum \( \frac{a}{1-r} \); the finite sum still includes the \( r^{N+1} \) tail correction.
This uses exponent \( N \) instead of \( N+1 \) in the finite-sum formula.
This has the wrong sign inside the numerator (swaps \( 1-r^{N+1} \) for \( r^{N+1}-1 \)).
This wrongly treats the sum as if the ratio were \( 1 \), just multiplying the first term by the number of terms.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.

solution: Partial fractions give \( \dfrac{1}{(k+[[a]])(k+[[a]]+1)} = \dfrac{1}{k+[[a]]} - \dfrac{1}{k+[[a]]+1} \). The sum telescopes to \( \dfrac{1}{1+[[a]]} - \dfrac{1}{[[N]]+[[a]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the value of the infinite sum \( \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \); the finite sum still subtracts the final tail term.
This is only the subtracted tail term \( \dfrac{1}{[[N]]+[[a]]+1} \), not the whole telescoped difference.
This adds the tail term instead of subtracting it.
This doubles the leading term of the telescoped expression.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.

solution: The \( N \)th partial sum telescopes to \( S_N = \dfrac{1}{[[a]]+1} - \dfrac{1}{N+[[a]]+1} \). As \( N \to \infty \) the second term vanishes, so the infinite sum is \( \displaystyle\lim_{N\to\infty} S_N = \dfrac{1}{[[a]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This squares the leading term instead of just taking its limiting value.
This inverts the correct answer, mistaking \( a+1 \) itself for its reciprocal.
This wrongly assumes the whole telescoped expression vanishes in the limit, not just the tail term.
This doubles the correct leading term.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A ball is dropped from a height of \( [[h]] \) meters. Each time it hits the ground, it rebounds to \( \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \) of the height it just fell from, bouncing forever. What is the total distance the ball travels (down and up, summed over all bounces)?
solution: After the initial drop of \( [[h]] \), the ball travels up and down \( 2\cdot[[h]]r \), then \( 2\cdot[[h]]r^{2} \), and so on, with \( r=\frac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \), an infinite geometric series. The total is \( [[h]] + \dfrac{2\cdot[[h]]r}{1-r} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the initial drop is not doubled, treating the whole motion as a single geometric series without the leading \( [[h]] \).
This omits both the initial drop term and the factor of \( 2 \) for up-and-down travel.
This forgets the factor of \( 2 \): each bounce after the first involves both a rise and a fall.
This doubles the initial drop itself instead of only the bounce distances.
This is off by one from the correct total distance.
This is off by one from the correct total distance.

solution: Each term is \( \ln(k+[[c]]) - \ln(k+[[c]]-1) \), so the sum telescopes: \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{[[N]]}\left[\ln(k+[[c]])-\ln(k+[[c]]-1)\right] = \ln([[N]]+[[c]]) - \ln([[c]]) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{[[N]]+[[c]]}{[[c]]}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to subtract the head term \( \ln([[c]]) \).
This is only the (negative of the) head term, not the full telescoped difference.
This uses \( \ln([[N]]) \) instead of the correctly telescoped \( \ln([[N]]+[[c]])-\ln([[c]]) \).
This multiplies instead of using the log-difference telescoping identity.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
